algorithms.title: ACLS Cardiac Arrest Rhythm Identification and Treatment Pathway
algorithms.description: Systematic approach to identifying and treating the four cardiac arrest rhythms during ACLS resuscitation.
steps: Cardiac arrest recognized — begin high-quality CPR and apply defibrillator pads.
At the first opportunity, analyze the cardiac rhythm on the monitor.
RHYTHM IDENTIFICATION:
→ VF: chaotic, disorganized activity with no identifiable QRS complexes → SHOCKABLE
→ Pulseless VT: rapid, regular, wide-complex tachycardia (>150/min) with no pulse → SHOCKABLE
→ Asystole: flat line (no ventricular activity) in at least two leads with proper settings → NON-SHOCKABLE
→ PEA: any organized rhythm (sinus, bradycardia, junctional, etc.) without a palpable pulse → NON-SHOCKABLE
SHOCKABLE PATHWAY (VF / pVT):
→ Defibrillate (biphasic 120–200J; monophasic 360J)
→ Resume CPR immediately for 2 minutes
→ Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO after the second shock
→ Consider amiodarone 300 mg IV (first dose) or lidocaine 1–1.5 mg/kg for refractory VF/pVT
NON-SHOCKABLE PATHWAY (Asystole / PEA):
→ Continue CPR — do NOT defibrillate
→ Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO as soon as feasible, repeat every 3–5 minutes
→ Systematically identify and treat reversible causes (H's and T's)
After every 2-minute cycle: reanalyze rhythm → treat accordingly
If ROSC achieved (sustained ETCO2 ≥40 mmHg or pulse confirmed): transition to post-arrest care

algorithms.title: Wide-Complex Tachycardia Evaluation
algorithms.description: Approach to evaluating and managing a wide-complex tachycardia during ACLS.
steps: Wide-complex tachycardia identified on monitor.
STEP 1: Is the patient pulseless?
→ YES: Treat as pulseless VT → Defibrillate (120–200J biphasic) and begin CPR.
→ NO: Patient has a pulse → proceed to STEP 2.
STEP 2: Is the patient hemodynamically unstable? (hypotension, altered mental status, acute heart failure, chest pain)
→ YES: Immediate synchronized cardioversion starting at 100J (monomorphic VT).
→ NO: Proceed to STEP 3.
STEP 3: Attempt vagal maneuvers and adenosine for possible SVT.
→ If responds to adenosine: SVT with aberrancy confirmed.
→ If no response: likely VT → consider amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 min or procainamide 20–50 mg/min, or synchronized cardioversion.
Clinical features favoring VT: prior MI or structural heart disease, AV dissociation, fusion/capture beats, concordant precordial QRS concordance.
When uncertain: treat as VT — it is the safer assumption.
